it.tri_ptil_ezi_d: Esclusivamente di origine acquisita, causata da stress meccanici ripetuti e degrado chimico-fisico dell'estremità del capello.
it.tri_clas_mor_d: Frattura trasversale incompleta che interessa midollo e corteccia, ma con risparmio della cuticola (che rimane intatta a "ponte" sul punto di frattura). Nota anche come frattura "a legno verde" (greenstick fracture).
it.tri_clas_ezi_d: Conseguenza di traumi fisico-chimici lievi o trazioni moderate che non riescono a rompere completamente la fibra esterna.
it.moni_mor_d: Fusto senza torsioni, con rigonfiamenti ellittici (nodi) alternati a restringimenti affusolati (internodi). La cuticola è assente nei nodi e alterata negli internodi, dove avvengono le fratture.
it.moni_ezi_d: Difetto genetico nella zona cheratogenetica del follicolo, con alternanza di aree allargate e ristrette.
it.pseudo_moni_l4: Fratture si verificano quasi esclusivamente nei nodi.
it.pili_torti_mor_d: Fusto con avvolgimenti su sé stesso (3–5 torsioni regolari). La sezione diventa ellittica nei punti ritorti. Estremamente fragili (lunghezza < 5 cm).
it.pili_torti_ezi_d: Anomalia ereditaria rara (X-linked recessiva), legata a mutazioni nel gene PLS3. Può essere acquisita in seguito a traumi ripetuti.
it.pili_torti_diag_d: Rifrazione irregolare alla luce incidente. Associata a Sindrome di Menkes (carenza di rame).
it.cap_aff_mor_d: Presenza di restringimenti progressivi lungo il fusto.
it.cap_aff_ezi_d: Causata da rallentamento temporaneo della matrice follicolare. Associata ad alopecia areata, tricotillomania, malattie sistemiche gravi o farmaci citotossici.
it.scanalature_longitudinali_paragrafo: Difetti zonali nella crescita follicolare con riduzione dello spessore della corteccia in corrispondenza della scanalatura, ma cuticola intatta. Non patologiche di per sé, ma spesso associate a tricotiodistrofia, moniletrix o pili torti.
it.pili_annulati_morfologia: Fusto con bande alternate chiare e scure (aspetto "zebrato") dovute a spazi d'aria tra cellule corticali non allineate. Effetto luccicante esteticamente gradevole.
it.pili_annulati_eziologia: Di solito ereditaria (autosomica dominante), ma può essere sporadica.
it.pili_bifurcati_morfologia: Il fusto si divide in due rami, ciascuno con propria cuticola, per poi ricongiungersi. Differisce dalla tricoptilòsi, che è una frattura longitudinale.
it.pili_bifurcati_eziologia: Ipotesi di una matrice secondaria temporanea adiacente al follicolo principale.
it.pili_multigemini_morfologia: Anomalia rara con 2–10 peli di sezione irregolare prodotti da matrici multiple ma che emergono da un unico ostio follicolare.
it.pili_multigemini_caratteristiche: Ogni pelo ha guaine epiteliali interne proprie; la guaina esterna è unica. Tutti i peli sono solitamente in fase anagen. Distinto dalla tricostàsi spinulosa.
it.anomalie_residue_titolo: ANOMALIE RESIDUE E ARRICCIATURE DEL FUSTO ➰
it.triconodosi_paragrafo: Anomalia acquisita in cui il fusto si presenta annodato su sé stesso. A questo livello, la cuticola è alterata o assente a causa del continuo sfregamento.
it.triconodosi_patogenesi: Patogenesi: Traumatica (legature strette, acconciature, capelli lunghi non spazzolati).
it.triconodosi_frequenza: Frequenza: Più comune nei soggetti con capelli ricci o molto fini.
it.capelli_impetinabili_paragrafo: Alterazione solitamente ereditaria (mutazioni nei geni PADI3, TGM3, TCHH) in cui i capelli, di colore giallo-grigiastro, non possono essere pettinati né appiattiti.
it.capelli_impetinabili_morfologia: Morfologia: Stelo con sezione triangolare e scanalature longitudinali lungo i lati.
it.capelli_impetinabili_eziologia: Eziologia: Cheratinizzazione prematura della guaina epiteliale interna che deforma il capello in crescita.
it.capelli_lanosi_paragrafo: Fusti a sezione ovoidale con curvature o torsioni assiali che causano un forte arricciamento. Simili ai pili torti, ma generalmente meno fragili.
it.capelli_lanosi_caratteristiche: Caratteristiche: Non pettinabili. Fase anagen ridotta, limita la crescita a pochi centimetri.
it.seborrea_paragrafo: La seborrea è la produzione di sebo in eccesso che altera l'equilibrio idro-lipidico della cute, causando inestetismi o patologie (es. acne, caduta di capelli).
it.seborrea_fisiologica: Fisiologica: Normale attività che crea il mantello idro-lipidico (protezione) e lubrifica il fusto.
it.seborrea_patologica: Patologica: Produzione anormale che rende cute e capelli grassi e oleosi. Può portare a pelle asfittica (sebo ristagna nei follicoli, cute secca e ruvida) e comedoni.
it.seborrea_occasionale: Occasionale: Ipersecrezione limitata a brevi periodi (es. da fattori ambientali o trattamenti errati).
it.seborrea_secca: Seborrea secca: Pelle con eccesso di sebo sottostante ma superficie secca e squamosa.
it.seborrea_cause_interne: Squilibri ormonali (androgeni), disturbi digestivi, fattori genetici, stress.
it.seborrea_cause_esterne: Fattori ambientali, prassi igieniche scorrette (es. prodotti troppo alcalini), lozioni alcoliche, massaggi intensi, alimentazione ricca di zuccheri e latticini.
it.seborrea_fattori_microbici: Proliferazione di lieviti Malassezia e batteri Cutibacterium acnes, che degradano il sebo in acidi irritanti.
it.seborrea_alopecia: Alopecia Seborroica: Caduta di capelli e accelerazione della calvizie.
it.seborrea_patologie_cutanee: Patologie Cutanee: Follicolite, acne o comedoni (per ostruzione follicolare).
it.seborrea_pitiriasi: Pitiriasi Steatoide: Seborrea accompagnata da forfora (squame untuose e giallastre).
it.seborrea_dermatite: Dermatite Seborroica: Infiammazione acuta con perdita di squame untuose sul cuoio capelluto, sopracciglia, naso, ascelle.
it.seborrea_cisti_sebacee: Cisti Sebacee: Accumulo di sebo e cheratina in aree come cuoio capelluto e tronco.
it.seborrea_terapia_paragrafo: Trattamenti antiseborroici e sebo-equilibranti, talvolta associati a farmaci (imidazolici, corticosteroidi, antiandrogeni). Utile l'associazione con trattamenti biostimolanti come l'Alta Frequenza (disinfetta e vascolarizza) o la Laserterapia (rinforza i capelli). È importante evitare detergenti aggressivi che inducono effetto rebound.
it.forfora_paragrafo: La forfora è causata da un accelerato ricambio delle cellule epidermiche (ciclo di 10–12 giorni anziché 28), che non raggiungono la completa maturazione prima del distacco.
it.forfora_fattori_titolo: Fattori Scatenanti e Ruolo del Micete
it.forfora_morfologia: Morfologia: Ammassi di cellule cornee (squame bianche o grigiastre).
it.forfora_permeabilita: Permeabilità: Lo strato corneo è più permeabile del normale, favorendo l'assorbimento di principi attivi.
it.forfora_malassezia: Malassezia: Lievito presente in eccesso. Un pH alterato e il sebo in eccesso favoriscono la sua crescita, che a sua volta stimola infiammazione e desquamazione.
it.forfora_secca: Pitiriasi Secca (Semplice): Squame fini e bianche, cute normale, prurito lieve.
it.forfora_grassa: Pitiriasi Grassa (Steatoide): Squame grosse, untuose e giallastre, cute eritematosa e oleosa, spesso associata a seborrea.
it.forfora_terapia_antimicotica: L'obiettivo è riequilibrare il microbiota e controllare la proliferazione di Malassezia con prodotti ad azione antimicotica (es. ketoconazolo, piritione di zinco, acido salicilico, tea tree oil).
it.forfora_terapia_ph: Lo shampoo deve avere un pH acido (4–5,5) per ripristinare il manto acido del cuoio capelluto e creare un ambiente sfavorevole al fungo. Lasciarlo in posa 3–5 minuti prima del risciacquo per massimizzare l'efficacia.
it.simple_title_main: CAPELLI: COME SONO FATTI E IL LORO CICLO VITALE
it.simple_anatomy_p1: I peli e i capelli sono filamenti fatti di cheratina. Nascono da un "sacco" nella pelle chiamato FOLLICOLO PILIFERO.
it.simple_anatomy_p2: Il follicolo si trova sotto la pelle del cuoio capelluto (derma). La sua parte finale, concava, si chiama BULBO.
it.simple_anatomy_p3: La lunghezza, lo spessore e la posizione dei capelli cambiano in base a dove si trovano, al sesso, all'età e alla razza.
it.simple_part_fusto: La parte che esce dalla pelle si chiama FUSTO (o STELO).
it.simple_part_radice: La parte sotto la pelle (dentro il follicolo) si chiama RADICE.
it.simple_part_papilla: In fondo alla radice c'è la PAPILLA DERMICA. Questa papilla porta il nutrimento al capello.
it.simple_part_matrice: Tra il bulbo e la radice c'è la MATRICE DEL CAPELLO. La matrice crea tutti i componenti del capello.
it.simple_follicle_text1: Il follicolo è un "sacco" della pelle, inclinato, che scende nel derma per 1-3 mm. Contiene il pelo e le sue guaine.
it.simple_follicle_text2: Ogni persona ha circa 5 milioni di follicoli. Circa 100.000 sono sul cuoio capelluto. Questo numero è stabilito prima della nascita e non aumenta più.
it.simple_gland_text1: Questa ghiandola produce il sebo, una sostanza grassa (trigliceridi, colesterolo, ecc.).
it.simple_gland_text2: Il sebo serve a lubrificare, ammorbidire e proteggere il capello man mano che cresce. Il sebo si diffonde sulla pelle e sul capello. La sua attività è controllata da fattori ormonali e alimentari.
it.simple_muscle_text1: È fatto di fibre muscolari lisce. Si attacca al follicolo.
it.simple_muscle_text2: Quando si contrae (per freddo o emozioni come paura), il pelo si mette dritto. Questo causa la pelle d'oca. Aiuta anche a far uscire il sebo.
it.simple_structure_p1: Il capello è una fibra complessa. Contiene acqua (10-30%) e grassi (sebo, cere, ecc.) per proteggersi.
it.simple_structure_p2: Il fusto del capello ha tre strati:
it.simple_layer_cuticola: CUTICOLA: È la parte più esterna. È fatta di cellule piatte e sovrapposte, come le tegole di un tetto.
it.simple_layer_corteccia: CORTECCIA: Si trova sotto la cuticola. È la parte più grande del capello. Dà al capello la sua resistenza e contiene la melanina (colore).
it.simple_layer_midollo: MIDOLLO: È la parte centrale, quasi assente nel fusto.
it.simple_structure_p3: Le molecole principali sono: cheratina (struttura), melanina (colore) e minerali.
it.simple_keratin_p1: Il capello è fatto principalmente di cheratina. Ne esistono due tipi:
it.simple_keratin_filamentosa: Cheratina Filamentosa: Forma dei fasci (fibrille) nella corteccia.
it.simple_keratin_amorfa: Cheratina Amorfa: Riempie gli spazi tra i fasci, dando stabilità.
it.simple_stability_p1: Le catene di cheratina sono unite da dei "ponti" che le tengono insieme. Ci sono 3 tipi di ponti:
it.simple_bridge_hydrogen_text: Sono legami molto deboli. Si rompono facilmente con l'acqua o il calore. Danno al capello flessibilità.
it.simple_bridge_saline_text: Sono legami mediamente forti. Si rompono con forte calore o con sostanze acide/basiche. Danno al capello resistenza.
it.simple_bridge_disulfide_text1: Sono legami molto forti (legami covalenti), fatti da atomi di zolfo.
it.simple_bridge_disulfide_text2: Questi ponti danno struttura (ad esempio i capelli ricci naturali). Possono essere rotti solo con molta energia (es. trattamenti chimici).
it.simple_bridge_disulfide_text3: I tre tipi di legame contribuiscono tutti alla resistenza del capello.
it.simple_minerals_p1: I minerali sono essenziali. Se mancano, i capelli diventano fragili, secchi e opachi.
it.simple_mineral_ferro: FERRO: Se manca, causa fragilità e caduta eccessiva.
it.simple_mineral_rame: RAME: Fondamentale per il colore (melanina) e per i ponti disolfuro.
it.simple_mineral_zinco: ZINCO: Indispensabile per la crescita delle cellule.
it.simple_lipids_p1: I lipidi provengono per lo più dal sebo.
it.simple_lipids_p2: I fosfolipidi sono importanti perché aiutano a mantenere la corretta idratazione del capello.
it.simple_melanin_p1: La melanina è il pigmento naturale. Si trova nella corteccia ed è prodotta dai melanociti.
it.simple_melanin_feomelanin: FEOMELANINA: Colore giallo/rosso (capelli biondi e rossi).
it.simple_melanin_tricochromes: TRICOCROMI: Pigmenti speciali per il colore rosso intenso.
it.simple_melanin_p3: La melanina nasce dall'aminoacido tirosina. La diversa percentuale dei pigmenti determina il colore finale del capello.
it.simple_cycle_p1: Il capello ha cicli di crescita e di riposo. Quando un capello vecchio cade, il follicolo ne forma uno nuovo.
it.simple_anagen_p1: È la fase più lunga. La matrice del capello è molto attiva e lo fa crescere.
it.simple_anagen_growth: Il capello cresce di circa 1-1,5 cm al mese.
it.simple_catagen_p1: È molto breve, dura 2-4 settimane. La matrice smette di lavorare.
it.simple_telogen_p1: È il periodo di riposo, dura circa 3 mesi.
it.simple_telogen_p2: Il capello cade solo quando ne sta crescendo uno nuovo che lo spinge fuori.
it.simple_figure_cycle_caption: Le tre fasi del ciclo di vita del capello.
it.simple_cycle_p3: La caduta dei capelli nell'uomo è a "mosaico": ogni follicolo segue il suo ciclo da solo. Questo assicura che non ci siano periodi senza capelli.
it.simple_cycle_note: Nota: ogni follicolo può fare fino a 30 cicli di vita.

en.title_main: HAIR: ANATOMY, STRUCTURE, AND LIFE CYCLE
en.anatomy_p1: Hairs are filamentous and keratinized cutaneous appendages. They originate from HAIR FOLLICLES, oblique invaginations of the epidermis that extend deep into the dermis, terminating at the base with an expanded, concave structure: the HAIR BULB.
en.anatomy_p2: Their length, thickness, and topographic distribution vary according to anatomical site, sex, age, and the individual's ethnicity.
en.anatomy_p3: Anatomically, the hair is divided into three portions: the SHAFT, which is the visible segment emerging on the skin surface; the ROOT, the internal section housed within the follicle; and the BULB, the deepest portion. Within the cavity of the bulb lies the DERMAL PAPILLA, a highly vascularized and innervated conical connective tissue structure responsible for nourishing the hair.
en.anatomy_p4: The lower portion of the bulb surrounding the papilla constitutes the HAIR MATRIX (or germinal layer): a population of undifferentiated cells with high mitotic activity, responsible for the synthesis and progressive keratinization of all hair components.
en.anatomy_p5: Together with the associated glandular and muscular structures, the follicle and hair constitute the morpho-functional unit known as the PILOSEBACEOUS APPARATUS.
en.figure1_caption: Anatomical section of the PILOSEBACEOUS APPARATUS.
en.comp_follicolo: HAIR FOLLICLE, containing the hair shaft and its respective sheaths;
en.comp_ghiandola: SEBACEOUS GLAND (associated with an apocrine sweat gland in specific anatomical sites).
en.follicle_text: Is a specialized epidermal structure, obliquely oriented relative to the skin surface, which extends deep into the dermis to a depth of 1-3 mm. The total number of follicles in humans is genetically determined, totaling approximately 5 million, with about 100,000 located on the scalp. Follicular morphogenesis is an early process: it begins between the 9th and 12th weeks of gestation and is completed by the 7th month of fetal life, after which no new follicles are formed de novo.
en.gland_text: Is a holocrine exocrine gland that releases its secretion, sebum, into the follicular infundibulum via the sebaceous duct. Sebum, composed of a lipid mixture rich in triglycerides, squalene, wax esters, and cholesterol, lubricates, hydrates, and waterproofs the hair cuticle during growth, protecting the skin. Being localized in the upper portion of the follicle, its activity does not interfere with the mitotic processes of the bulb matrix. Sebaceous secretion is finely regulated by endocrine factors (primarily androgens), as well as nervous and metabolic stimuli.
en.muscle_text: Consisting of a bundle of smooth muscle fibers, it anchors to the papillary dermis and inserts onto the connective tissue sheath of the follicle, above the sebaceous gland. Its involuntary contraction, mediated by the sympathetic nervous system in response to cold or emotional stimuli (fear, stress), causes the verticalization of the hair shaft (goosebumps). This movement exerts mechanical pressure on the underlying sebaceous gland, actively promoting the expulsion of sebum into the follicular lumen.
en.secHeader_structure: PILOSEBACEOUS APPARATUS IN DETAIL - THE HAIR: STRUCTURE
en.structure_p1: The hair is a complex biological structure with a high degree of organization, composed of specific macromolecular and chemical constituents. Its hydration level varies from 10% to 30% by weight, closely related to environmental thermo-hygrometric conditions. The cuticular surface is coated with a lipid sebum film (comprising triglycerides, waxes, cholesterol, phospholipids, and squalene) that acts as an essential barrier against external chemical and physical insults.
en.structure_p2: The hair shaft is structured into three concentric layers:
en.layer_cuticola: Cuticle: the outermost layer, composed of flattened and highly keratinized cells (up to 6-8 overlapping cellular tiers), arranged longitudinally like roof tiles to protect the underlying cortex.
en.layer_corteccia: Cortex: located beneath the cuticle, it represents the quantitative majority of the shaft. It is formed by spindle-shaped cells rich in aligned keratin filaments and determines the mechanical properties of hair strength and elasticity. It also houses melanin granules, responsible for pigmentation.
en.layer_midollo: Medulla: the central core of the shaft, often discontinuous or entirely absent in fine hair. It consists of loosely connected keratinized cells arranged in rows and interspersed with air vacuoles (microscopic air spaces) and traces of pigment.
en.figure2_caption: Detailed representation of the complete Pilosebaceous Apparatus.
en.structure_p3: From a biochemical perspective, the fundamental constituents of hair (excluding water) include keratin (predominantly in the hydrophobic alpha conformation), structural and surface lipids, melanin pigments (eumelanins and pheomelanins), and a fraction of essential atomic-state trace elements and minerals (such as iron, zinc, magnesium, and calcium).
en.chem_analysis_p: The structure of the hair is quite complex in its organization; the chemical analysis of the hair indicates the main constituents, besides water, of the hair: keratin, lipids, minerals, and pigments.
en.keratin_p1: In reality, there are two keratins in hair, which are defined by their structural characteristics as filamentous keratin and amorphous keratin. Filamentous keratin forms a complex ultra-structure organized into bundles or fibrils, which are found in the hair cortex, while amorphous keratin fills the empty spaces between the fibrils, stabilizing the structure. The joined chains wrap around themselves, taking on a spiral shape similar to that of DNA.
en.stability_secHeader: HOW DOES THIS ULTRASTRUCTURE REMAIN STABLE?
en.stability_p1: Keratin macromolecules, consisting of long polypeptide chains, are stabilized and laterally cohesive due to interchain chemical bonds (cross-links) established between the amino acid residues of adjacent filaments.
en.stability_p2: Three main types of structural interactions can be distinguished:
en.bridge_hydrogen_text: These are weak electrostatic interactions, easily broken by the action of water and heat (the phenomenon underlying transient thermal or wet hair styling). They are primarily established between the oxygen atom of the carbonyl group (-C=O) and the hydrogen atom of the amide group (-NH-) of the peptide backbone. Intrachain bonds stabilize the α-helix conformation; interchain bonds contribute to providing flexibility and reversible elasticity to the hair fiber.
en.bridge_saline_text: These are medium-energy bonds, sensitive to extreme temperature variations and, above all, to sudden changes in pH (highly acidic or basic solutions). They are electrostatic interactions that occur between the ionized functional groups of the side chains of acidic (e.g., glutamic acid, aspartic acid) and basic (e.g., lysine, arginine) amino acids belonging to adjacent α-helices. Numerically superior to disulfide bonds, they are essential for structural stability and tensile strength.
en.bridge_disulfide_p1: These are strong covalent bonds, formed by the oxidation of the thiol groups (-SH) of two cysteine residues located on adjacent polypeptide chains, resulting in the formation of a cystine molecule (-S-S-). These bonds stably cross-link both the internal structures of microfibrils (α-helices) and the surrounding amorphous protein matrix (rich in sulfur), ensuring the cohesion and biomechanical integrity of the entire hair ultrastructure.
en.bridge_disulfide_p2: The disulfide bond represents the primary factor stabilizing the tertiary and quaternary structure of keratin, determining the rigidity, chemical-physical resistance, and morphology (curvature) of hair and nails. In the cortex of naturally curly hair, the asymmetric arrangement and frequency of these bonds induce the protein chains to flex and bend permanently. The cleavage of these bonds requires energetic redox processes, industrially exploited in chemical structural modification treatments (perming, chemical straightening, bleaching, and oxidative hair coloring).
en.bridge_disulfide_p3: Although salt bridges and hydrogen bonds possess a lower bond energy compared to disulfide bonds, their extremely high numerical density means that each of the three bond classes contributes approximately 1/3 to the overall mechanical strength and shape stability of the hair.
en.bridge_disulfide_p4: The spatial configuration, density, and geometric regularity with which these three types of bonds are distributed among the keratin filaments (particularly the architecture of the disulfide bonds) determine the macromolecular organization of the cortex and, consequently, the morphological phenotype (straight, wavy, curly) of the hair.
en.minerals_p1: Minerals constitute essential biochemical and catalytic components for hair homeostasis. Mineral deficiency states directly reflect an alteration in keratogenesis, inducing structural fragility, dryness, loss of luster, and anomalies in the hair follicle cycle (telogen effluvium).
en.minerals_p2: The main trace elements involved are:
en.mineral_ferro: IRON (4-12 μg/g): An essential enzymatic co-factor; its deficiency (sideropenia) compromises cell proliferation in the bulb matrix, inducing fragility and effluvium. It exhibits typically high concentrations in hair containing pheomelanin (red).
en.mineral_magnesio: MAGNESIUM (30-45 μg/g): Acts as an activator in numerous enzymatic complexes and in ATP synthesis, which is fundamental for hair protein biosynthesis. It is more highly concentrated in dark hair.
en.mineral_rame: COPPER (16-50 μg/g): Constitutes the catalytic core of the tyrosinase enzyme, indispensable for melanogenesis. Furthermore, it indirectly participates in structural stability by catalyzing the formation of disulfide bonds in keratin.
en.mineral_zinco: ZINC (150-180 μg/g): A fundamental trace element for polymerase activity and division of germ cells in the bulb matrix. Chronic deficiency inhibits cell turnover, causing marked fragility and dystrophy of the shaft.
en.minerals_p3: The endogenous mineral component also helps regulate the intrinsic hydration of the hair fiber by maintaining hygroscopic balance; the loss of bound water alters the spatial arrangement of macrofibrils, compromising structural cohesion.
en.lipids_p1: Hair lipids are divided into exogenous (predominantly of sebaceous origin, consisting of triglycerides, waxes, squalene, and free fatty acids coating the cuticle) and endogenous (structural lipids integrated into the cell membrane and the CMC - Cell Membrane Complex, such as cholesterol and cholesterol sulfate).
en.lipids_p2: Of fundamental structural relevance are phospholipids and ceramides, intrinsic constituents of the hair cell membranes. Thanks to their amphipathic nature, these molecules modulate the hydrophobicity and permeability of the fiber, regulating water fluxes and ensuring proper hydration, elasticity, and intercellular cohesion between cuticular and cortical cells.
en.melanin_p1: Melanin is the natural pigmented polymer located predominantly in the cortex (the intermediate and volumetric layer of the hair). It is synthesized within melanosomes by melanocytes situated in the hair bulb, above the dermal papilla. The concentration, ratio, and density of these polymers determine the natural color spectrum of the hair (canities, or graying, is due to the cessation of melanogenesis and the consequent absence of pigment).
en.melanin_p2: Two main macromolecular classes can be distinguished:
en.melanin_list1: EUMELANIN and PHEOMELANIN: Eumelanin is a high-molecular-weight insoluble polymer, prevalent in dark phenotypes (brown and black). Pheomelanin is a sulfur-containing polymer, characteristic of light and warm shades (blonde and red).
en.melanin_list2: Red hair also presents specific polymeric variants historically known as TRICHOCHROMES (low-molecular-weight pigments containing iron, related to trichosiderin). The red hair phenotype (rutilism) is regulated by recessive variants of the melanocortina-1 receptor gene (MC1R).
en.melanin_bio_p1: The common biochemical precursor is the amino acid L-tyrosine. In eumelanin synthesis, tyrosine is hydroxylated into L-DOPA and subsequently oxidized to dopaquinone by the action of the key enzyme tyrosinase (a copper-dependent enzyme). Through subsequent stages of cyclization and oxidative polymerization, dopaquinone evolves into complex insoluble polymers. The catalytic efficiency of tyrosinase determines the intensity and saturation of the pigment, from light brown to absolute black.
en.melanin_bio_p2: The pathway shifts toward pheomelanin synthesis if a critical concentration of cysteine (or glutathione) is present within the melanosome. Cysteine reacts rapidly with dopaquinone to form cysteinyl-DOPA conjugates. These compounds undergo subsequent oxidations and polymerizations, generating a polymeric pigment whose coloration varies from the yellow to the orange-red spectrum based on the stoichiometric ratio of the precursors.
en.melanin_bio_p3: Oculocutaneous albinism represents a clinical model of dysfunction in this pathway: it is a genetic anomaly caused by mutations in the gene encoding the tyrosinase enzyme. Point or missense mutations alter the primary structure of the enzymatic protein (e.g., replacing key structural amino acids), causing total or partial loss of catalytic activity. The upstream metabolic block prevents the conversion of tyrosine, resulting in the complete absence of melanin synthesis.
en.melanin_p4: The natural coloration of the fiber is always the result of the co-presence and quantitative ratio between EUMELANIN, PHEOMELANIN, and TRICHOCHROMES.
en.melanin_list3_eumelanin: Eumelanin: organizes into large, granular, ellipsoidal melanosomes, distributed mainly in the peripheral region of the cortex. It selectively absorbs light, reflecting darker wavelengths; its dominance determines dark depth levels (black and brown).
en.melanin_list3_tricosiderina: Trichochromes: present an intermediate chemical structure and medium grain size, locating stably within the cortical matrix and providing the characteristic warm, coppery, and saturated reflections typical of red hair.
en.melanin_list3_feomelanina: Pheomelanin: occurs in the form of small, spherical, and partially diffused melanosomes (diffused pigment), distributed homogeneously even in the deeper areas of the cortex. It reflects yellow and orange wavelengths, determining the background of blonde and light hair.
en.melanin_p5: The reciprocal percentage variation and volumetric density of these polymeric fractions within the cortex genetically define the entire scale of tones and shades of natural hair.
en.cycle_p1: The hair follicle is a dynamic organ characterized by asynchronous cyclic activity, alternating between phases of growth, regression, and rest. Under physiological conditions, the shedding of a hair in the telogen phase coincides with the activation of a new anagen cycle within the same follicle. Polytrichia phenomena (multiple shafts emerging from a single follicular infundibulum) predominantly occur within the follicular units of the scalp, where distinct follicles share a common sebaceous gland and hair canal.
en.cycle_p2: The biological cycle of the hair is classically divided into three main phases: anagen, catagen, and telogen.
en.anagen_p1: Anagen is divided into 6 subphases (from anagen I to anagen VI). The biological process begins with the activation of stem cells within the bulge niche and the matrix, stimulating intense mitotic activity until the complete formation of the new hair shaft. On the scalp, the anagen phase lasts an average of 2-4 years in men and 3-7 years in women. Consequently, the loss of 'mature' hair is compensated for by the genesis of a new terminal strand. In other body regions, anagen is drastically shorter: 1-6 months for eyelashes and 20-40 days for body hair on limbs.
en.anagen_p2: The duration of anagen is genetically determined and regulates the maximum length the hair can reach. The kinetic growth rate is approximately 1-1.5 cm per month, with generally higher rates in females. This phase varies by ethnicity (reaching its maximum in straight, lissotrichous hair of Asian ethnicity) and scalp topography: follicles in the occipital region (nape) exhibit a longer anagen phase compared to temporal ones. Concurrently, the vertical proliferation rate is statistically higher at the vertex than at the nape.
en.figure3_caption: Schematic representation of the hair follicle life cycle phases.
en.anagen_p3: In follicles affected by androgenetic alopecia (pattern hair loss), there is a progressive temporal reduction of the anagen phase (down to just 3-5 months), associated with follicular miniaturization, which restricts the shaft length to a few centimeters and alters its structure into vellus hair. Biological aging (chrono-aging) also physiologically reduces anagen duration and shaft diameter.
en.anagen_note: Scientific Note: The replicative potential of the follicle is not mathematically predetermined at a local level, but is constrained by the stem cell reservoir and the organism's lifespan. Over an average human lifespan, a single follicle completes approximately 25-30 cycles before undergoing senescence.
en.catagen_text: This is a brief transitional regressive phase lasting 2-4 weeks. During catagen, matrix keratinocytes undergo programmed apoptosis, melanin synthesis ceases, and the lower portion of the follicle is resorbed, causing the dermal papilla to migrate upward toward the upper dermis (bulge area).
en.telogen_title: - Rest and Shedding Phase (TELOGEN and EXOGEN):
en.telogen_p1: Telogen represents the period of metabolic quiescence for the follicle, lasting an average of about 3 months. Mitotic activity is absent, and the anchoring sheaths become keratinized, giving the base of the hair its characteristic 'club' structure. The actual shedding of the shaft from the follicle (now independently classified as the exogen phase) occurs physiologically when the new anagen strand mechanically prompts its removal, ensuring the continuity of hair turn-over.
en.telogen_p2: If the follicle remains empty during a latent period between the shedding of the old hair and the onset of the new anagen, it is referred to as the kenogen phase. This empty interval, though present in a minimal percentage in a healthy scalp, undergoes a significant and pathological increase in androgenetic alopecia.
en.telogen_p3: The traumatic or mechanical extraction of a telogen hair induces a biochemical signal that interrupts follicular quiescence, stimulating an early onset of a new anagen phase. The characteristic whitish bulb visible to the naked eye at the base of spontaneously shed hair is not the 'living root', but rather the keratinized, unpigmented club end typical of the telogen phase.
en.telogen_p4: Unlike many mammals subject to synchronous shedding (molting), hair replacement in humans occurs according to an asynchronous 'mosaic' pattern: each follicle evolves independently from adjacent ones. Systemic fluctuations and photoperiod variations can, however, induce partial synchronization of the follicles, clinically evident as seasonal effluvium during transitional seasons (autumn and spring).
en.cycle_functional_title: From a strictly cyto-functional perspective, follicular activity is divided into two macro-phases:
en.cycle_functional_list1: A proliferative and morphogenetic phase: characterized by the rapid downward extension of the keratinocyte column invading the deep dermis, corresponding to the initial subphases of anagen (from anagen I to anagen V);
en.cycle_functional_list2: A differentative and synthesis phase: predominant during anagen VI, where mitotic activity stabilizes at the baseline level required to support continuous keratinization and protein synthesis of the shaft. The subsequent catagen represents mitotic arrest with terminal differentiation of residual cells, while telogen constitutes the biosynthetic silence necessary for the structural reorganization of the follicular unit.
en.cycle_functional_p: The temporal heterogeneity of the hair cycle across different anatomical areas (e.g., inverted anagen/telogen ratio in the pubic area and eyebrows compared to the scalp) accounts for the varying biological length of body hair and explains the selective susceptibility of follicles to specific dermatoses, immune-mediated, or hormone-dependent pathologies.
en.desc_atomo: The atom is the smallest particle of a chemical element that retains its specific characteristics and identity.
en.nucleo_desc: Nucleus: Composed of protons (positive charge) and neutrons (no charge). It contains almost all of the atom's mass (>99.9%).
en.elettroni_desc: Electrons: Negatively charged particles moving around the nucleus, occupying orbitals, regions of space where there is the highest probability of finding them (quantum mechanical model).
en.concetto_atomo_neutro: An electrically neutral atom has an equal number of protons and electrons, resulting in a net charge = 0.
en.desc_ione: An ION is an atom (or group of atoms) that has lost or gained one or more electrons, acquiring a net electric charge.
en.dd_anione: Formed by the gain of electrons; the resulting charge is negative.
en.dd_catione: Formed by the loss of electrons; the resulting charge is positive.
en.desc_tavola_periodica: Elements are ordered by increasing atomic number (Z), following the periodicity principle formulated by Mendeleev in 1869.
en.casella_simbolo: Center: the chemical symbol (e.g., Na, Cl).
en.casella_numero_atomico: Prominently displayed: the Atomic Number Z (number of protons; defines the element's identity).
en.casella_numero_massa: Usually at the bottom: the Relative Atomic Mass (expressed in a.m.u., it is the weighted average of the masses of its natural isotopes).
en.desc_legami_stabilita: Atoms join to form molecules or compounds that are more stable through chemical bonds.
en.concetto_energia_legame: A bond forms spontaneously because the resulting system has a lower energy content than the isolated atoms. Lower potential energy = greater stability.
en.desc_teoria_valenza: Atoms interact to achieve a stable outer electron configuration (minimum energy), similar to that of noble gases.
en.desc_regola_ottetto: Noble gases (Group 18) are chemically inert because they have a complete valence shell: 8 electrons (octet), except helium which is stable with 2 (duet).
en.regola_ottetto: Rule: “Atoms tend to lose, gain, or share electrons to achieve the electron configuration of the nearest noble gas (8 electrons in the outer shell, or 2 for H and He).”
en.desc_energia_legame: Bond energy is the energy required to break one mole of chemical bonds (in kJ/mol). It is a direct measure of bond strength: high values indicate strong bonds and thermodynamically stable molecules. The bond length is the equilibrium distance between the nuclei of the bonded atoms.
en.def_legame_ionico: Definition: Electrostatic force of attraction that establishes between oppositely charged ions. It typically forms between atoms with a high electronegativity difference (Δχ ≥ 1.7), resulting in an almost complete transfer of electron density.
en.ionico_passo1: The transfer of one or more valence electrons from one atom to another takes place.
en.ionico_passo2: The less electronegative atom (generally a metal) loses electrons, turning into a cation (positive ion).
en.ionico_passo3: The more electronegative atom (generally a non-metal) gains electrons, turning into an anion (negative ion).
en.ionico_caratteristiche: It does not form isolated molecules, but three-dimensional ordered geometric structures called crystal lattices (e.g., Sodium Chloride, NaCl).
en.caption_ionico: CRYSTAL LATTICE FORMED BY Na+ AND Cl- IONS HELD TOGETHER BY IONIC BONDS
en.desc_legame_covalente: It occurs through the sharing of electrons between non-metallic atoms with similar or identical electronegativity (△χ < 1.7).
en.caption_covalente: Covalent bond: sharing of valence electron pairs between atoms to achieve electronic stability.
en.concetto_legame_covalente: The two atoms **share one or more pairs (lone pairs) of electrons** within molecular orbitals, allowing each atom to complete its valence shell (electronic stability).
en.dd_covalente_puro: It forms between identical atoms or those with a negligible electronegativity difference (△χ ≤ 0.4). The electron cloud is symmetrically distributed between the two nuclei (e.g., H₂, O₂, CH₄).
en.dd_covalente_polare: It forms between atoms with different electronegativity (0.4 < △χ < 1.7). The bonding electrons are more strongly attracted to the more electronegative atom, generating a partial negative charge (δ⁻) on it and a partial positive charge (δ⁺) on the other. The molecule thus constitutes an electric dipole (e.g., HCl, H₂O).
en.dd_covalente_dativo: The shared pair of electrons originates entirely from a single atom (donor, possessing a lone pair) and is hosted in an empty orbital of the other atom (acceptor). Once formed, it is experimentally indistinguishable from a standard covalent bond; it is often graphically represented by an arrow (→) (e.g., H₃O⁺, NH₄⁺).
en.desc_legame_metallico: It is described by the "sea of electrons" model (or Drude-Lorentz model): the structure consists of a geometric lattice of fixed positive ions immersed in a diffuse cloud of highly delocalized valence electrons, free to move throughout the entire solid.
en.caption_metallico: Metallic bond model: metallic cations stabilized by a sea of delocalized electrons, responsible for conductivity and malleability properties.
en.concetto_legame_metallico: The cohesion of the metallic bond is ensured by the omnidirectional electrostatic attraction between the shared "mobile" valence electrons and the positive ionic cores.
en.desc_legami_deboli: Weak bonds have a significantly lower bond energy compared to interatomic (intramolecular) bonds, but they are fundamental in determining the macroscopic properties and physical state of molecular compounds.
en.inclusioni_vdw: Van der Waals forces: include dipole-dipole interactions (between polar molecules) and London dispersion forces (between non-polar molecules).
en.inclusioni_idrogeno: Hydrogen Bonding (a particularly strong type of dipole-dipole interaction).
en.desc_legame_idrogeno: It occurs when a hydrogen atom, covalently bonded to a highly electronegative and small element (such as fluorine, oxygen, or nitrogen), is attracted to the lone pair of electrons of another electronegative atom in a neighboring molecule.
en.caratteristiche_legame_idrogeno: It occurs in strongly polar molecules such as water (H2O) and ammonia (NH3). It is the strongest among intermolecular forces (around 20–50 kJ/mol), although it remains significantly weaker than an intramolecular covalent bond (e.g., the O-H bond requires about 460 kJ/mol to be cleaved).
en.effetti_legame_idrogeno: In ice, hydrogen bonds force the molecules to arrange themselves in a tetrahedral geometry that generates a very open hexagonal crystal lattice. This increases the volume and reduces the density of the solid compared to the liquid, allowing ice to float. In the liquid phase, these bonds continuously break and reform, giving water high cohesion, high surface tension, high specific heat, and high phase transition points.
en.def_reazione_chimica: A chemical reaction is a process involving the breaking of intramolecular bonds in reactants and the formation of new bonds in products, resulting in an atomic rearrangement.
en.desc_equazione_chimica:
A chemical equation is the qualitative and quantitative representation of a reaction, with reactants written on the left and products on the right:

REACTANTS → PRODUCTS
en.reazione_sintesi:
Synthesis: Two or more elementary substances or compounds combine to form a single, more complex compound.
Scheme: A + B → AB
en.reazione_scissione:
Decomposition (or Cleavage): A compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances, usually through the input of energy.
Scheme: AB → A + B
en.reazione_scambio_semplice:
Single Displacement (or Replacement): A free element replaces and displaces another element within a compound.
Scheme: A + BC → AC + B
en.reazione_scambio_doppio:
Double Displacement: Two compounds exchange their respective constituents (ions or atoms) to form two new compounds.
Scheme: AB + CD → AD + CB
en.reazione_reversibile: Reversible Reactions: Reactions in which products can react together to reform the reactants, reaching a state of dynamic chemical equilibrium. They are represented by a double reaction arrow: ⇌
en.reazione_irreversibile: Irreversible Reactions: Reactions that proceed almost completely toward the formation of products (until the limiting reactant is exhausted). The stable products do not tend to spontaneously recombine to restore the reactants.
en.def_composti_inorganici: These are chemical substances typically characterized by the absence of carbon-hydrogen (C-H) bonds. Unlike organic compounds, most do not contain carbon; however, certain carbon-containing species (such as carbon oxides, carbonates, bicarbonates, carbides, and cyanides) are classified as inorganic due to their chemical properties and mineral salt-like behavior.
en.ossidi_def: 1. Basic Oxides (Oxides): Binary compounds formed by a metal and oxygen.
en.ossidi_nomenclatura: Name: Oxide of + [Metal name]. If the metal has multiple oxidation states, it is specified using suffixes or Roman numerals (e.g., Iron(II) oxide or Ferrous oxide).
en.anidridi_def: 2. Acidic Oxides (Acid Anhydrides): Binary compounds formed by a non-metal and oxygen.
en.anidridi_nomenclatura: Name: Anhydride + [Non-metal name] + suffix (-ous/-ic or hypo-/per-). In modern IUPAC nomenclature: Oxide of + [non-metal] + numerical prefix.
en.idrossidi_formazione:
They derive from the hydration reaction of a basic oxide:
Basic Oxide + H2O → Hydroxide
en.idrossidi_caratteristica: The structure is characterized by the presence of one or more hydroxyl groups (OH<sup>-</sup>) bonded to a metal cation.
en.def_base_lowry: Brønsted-Lowry Definition: A base is a chemical species capable of accepting a proton (H<sup>+</sup>) from another species.
en.acidi_classificazione: Inorganic acids are classified based on the presence of oxygen:
en.acidi_ossigeno:
Oxoacids (Acidic oxides + H2O): They contain oxygen in their structure. They are formed by the reaction: Anhydride + H2O → Oxoacid.
Example: CO2 + H2O → H2CO3 (Carbonic acid).
en.acidi_idrogeno:
Hydracids / Binary Acids (Non-metals + H): They do not contain oxygen. The general formula is HnX, where X is a non-metal.
Example: HCl (Hydrochloric acid, in aqueous solution; hydrogen chloride in the gaseous state).
en.def_acido_lowry: Brønsted-Lowry Definition: An acid is a chemical species capable of donating a proton (H<sup>+</sup>) to another species.
en.sali_def: Salts are neutral ionic compounds resulting from the neutralization reaction between an acid and a base.
en.esempio_neutralizzazione:
Example:
HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H2O
(Hydrochloric acid + Sodium hydroxide → Sodium chloride + Water)
en.def_miscele: A mixture is a physicochemical system consisting of the coexistence of two or more pure substances that do not react with each other. Dispersed systems, defined by the presence of a dispersing phase (continuous) and a dispersed phase, are classified based on the degree of dispersion and thermodynamic homogeneity:
en.miscele_eterogenee: Heterogeneous Systems (Heterogeneous Mixtures and Colloids): Polyphasic systems in which the individual constituents retain their physical interfaces and macroscopic properties. They include suspensions, emulsions, foams, aerosols (mists and smokes), and colloidal systems.
en.miscele_omogenee: Homogeneous Systems (True Solutions): Monophasic systems at the molecular or ionic level, in which the individual components are neither optically nor physically distinguishable.
en.dd_sospensioni: Kinetically stable biphasic systems consisting of a solid dispersed phase (particle diameter > 1 µm) insoluble in a liquid dispersing phase. The particles are subject to gravitational sedimentation, described by Stokes' law, which can be countered by modifying the viscosity of the continuous medium.
en.dd_emulsioni: Thermodynamically unstable systems composed of two immiscible or partially miscible liquid phases. They require the action of emulsifying agents (surfactants) that reduce interfacial tension and prevent instability phenomena (coalescence, flocculation, creaming).
en.emulsione_o_a: O/W (Oil in Water): Lipophilic phase (discontinuous) dispersed in a hydrophilic phase (continuous). Characteristic of stable hydrating emulsions, hydrophilic cosmetics, and rapid-absorption systems.
en.emulsione_a_o: W/O (Water in Oil): Hydrophilic phase (discontinuous) dispersed in a lipophilic phase (continuous). Typical systems for barrier creams, ointments, and high-persistence cutaneous formulations.
en.dd_schiume: Fluid dispersed systems in which the dispersing phase is liquid (or solid) and the dispersed phase is gaseous, stabilized by surfactant interfacial films.
en.dd_nebbie_fumi: Systems in which the dispersing phase is a gas. Mist is a liquid aerosol (dispersed droplets), while smoke is a solid aerosol (dispersed solid particulate matter).
en.esempio_applicativo_testo: The structured emulsifying network (Polyglyceryl-4-Stearate, Cetearyl Glucoside) stabilizes the thermodynamically unstable interface of the O/W system. Amphiphilic consistency agents, such as Cetyl Alcohol, co-emulsify and modify the rheology of the system, while Cera Alba acts as a lipid consistency factor, modifying the rheological behavior and final texture.
en.dd_soluzioni_vere: Thermodynamically stable homogeneous (monophasic) systems in which the solute is dispersed within the solvent at the molecular or ionic level (dimensions < 1 nm).
en.dd_solubilita:
Solubility represents the maximum concentration of solute that can dissolve in the solvent at a defined temperature and pressure, reaching thermodynamic equilibrium. A solution is defined as saturated when a dynamic equilibrium is established between the dissolved solute and the undissolved residue.

NB: The dissolution process is thermodynamically temperature-dependent: generally endothermic for solids (solubility increases with T) and exothermic for gases.
en.dd_colloidi: Micro-heterogeneous systems characterized by particles with linear dimensions between 1 and 1000 nm. While showing prolonged kinetic stability and macroscopic uniformity, they possess a very high interfacial free energy.
en.dd_effetto_tyndall: They exhibit unique optical properties, including the Tyndall effect (light scattering or Rayleigh/Mie scattering by particles dispersed within the dispersed phase).
en.dd_tipi_colloidi: Classification: Lyophilic (high affinity and solvation of the dispersed phase, thermodynamically stable), lyophobic (poor affinity, unstable unless stabilized by electrostatic charges or steric barriers), and association colloids.
en.desc_colloidi_associazione: These are systems generated by the spontaneous self-aggregation of amphiphilic molecules (surfactants) possessing a hydrophilic portion (polar head) and a lipophilic portion (non-polar tail). Upon reaching the Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC), these molecules organize into aggregated structures called micelles, reducing the free energy of the system.
en.colloidi_gel: GEL: A biphasic colloidal system with high viscosity, in which the solid phase forms a continuous three-dimensional network that traps and immobilizes the liquid phase (viscoelastic behavior).
en.colloidi_sol: SOL: A fluid colloidal suspension in which solid particles are freely dispersed in the continuous liquid phase.
en.desc_ph: The pH (potential of hydrogen) is a logarithmic scale measuring the acidity or alkalinity of aqueous solutions, defined as the negative logarithm of the hydrogen ion activity (H+). It indicates the ratio between hydrogen ion (H+) and hydroxide ion (OH−) concentrations.
en.scala_valori: Scale: Generally from 0 (maximum acidity) to 14 (maximum alkalinity) in aqueous solutions at 25°C.
en.scala_acido: Acidic: pH < 7 ([H+] > [OH−]). An acid is a chemical species capable of donating protons (H+) to the solvent.
en.scala_alcalino: Alkaline (or Basic): pH > 7 ([H+] < [OH−]). A base is a chemical species capable of accepting protons (H+) from the solvent.
en.rilevanza_biologia_testo1: Even minor pH variations are critical for the human body, which is composed of approximately 70% aqueous solutions. Metabolic reactions and enzymatic activity depend strictly on maintaining ion concentrations ([H<sup>+</sup>] and [OH<sup>−</sup>]) within narrow physiological ranges (homeostasis).
en.rilevanza_biologia_testo2: A physiological blood pH of approximately 7.4 (range: 7.37–7.43) is essential for the structure and function of proteins and cell membranes. Alterations in intracellular pH and organelle pH (e.g., endosomes and lysosomes) are correlated with pathologies such as neoplasms, neurodegenerative diseases, and metabolic disorders. The endo-lysosomal system requires progressive acidification for the correct disposal of cellular waste.
en.desc_ph_cutaneo: Cosmetics, applied to the skin surface (in compliance with the EU Cosmetics Regulation), interact directly with the stratum corneum and the hydrolipidic film. It is therefore essential that their pH is compatible with the physiological pH of the skin to ensure eudermy (non-irritancy and respect for the barrier).
en.subtitle_manto_acido: The Acid Mantle and Skin Barrier
en.desc_manto_acido: The epidermis is protected by a surface acid mantle that serves as a primary defense mechanism against the onset of pathogenic microorganisms and dehydration.
en.dd_ambiente_alcalino: Favors the proliferation of pathogenic microorganisms. The bacterium responsible for acne, Cutibacterium acnes, thrives in environments with pH > 5.5. An alkaline pH compromises the integrity of the skin barrier, alters the hydrolipidic film, induces dehydration, dryness, hypersensitivity, and accelerates skin aging processes.
en.dd_ambiente_acido: Can alter the commensal bacterial flora (skin microbiota), weakening the natural biological defense. It can cause irritation, erythema, itching, and impairment of barrier function.
en.desc_ph_ideale: Determining the ideal pH is fundamental for skin integrity. Below are the optimal (eudermic) pH values for the main categories of cosmetics:
en.desc_regolazione_ph: pH regulation requires specific agents based on the desired correction direction. To raise pH (alkalization), strong bases such as Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH) or Potassium Hydroxide (KOH) are used, or buffers like Sodium Bicarbonate (effective up to pH ~8.3). To lower pH (acidification), weak organic acids such as Citric Acid, Lactic Acid, or Glycolic Acid are employed. The use of vinegar (Acetic Acid) is discouraged in stable formulations due to organic impurities.
en.note_regolazione_ph: Corrections require precision: add the adjusting agent dropwise under stirring, measuring pH after each aliquot to avoid overshoot. The safest and most effective standardized stock solutions are:
en.soluzione_acido_citrico: Citric Acid 10-20%: (10-20 g in 80-90 mL distilled water).
en.soluzione_soda_caustica: Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH) 10-18%: (10-18 g in 80-90 mL distilled water). Note: Handle with PPE (gloves, goggles) as it is highly corrosive.
en.soluzione_bicarbonato: Sodium Bicarbonate 5-10%: (5-10 g in 90-95 mL water). Useful as a light buffer.
en.title_ph_capelli: pH and Hair: Electrostatics and Cuticle Integrity 💇
en.desc_ph_capelli: Hair shaft health is governed by pH, which determines the net electric charge of the surface and the state of cuticle scale opening. Hair possesses an isoelectric point (pI) between 3.67 and 5.6, where positive and negative charges are balanced.
en.capello_sano: Physiological pH (4.5–5.5): In this slightly acidic range, the net charge is near zero or weakly positive. Electrostatic repulsions are minimal.
en.struttura_cuticola: Structural Integrity: Cuticle scales adhere tightly to the shaft ("closed" configuration). This reduces friction, maximizes light refraction (shine), and limits internal water and protein loss.
en.desc_danneggiamento_alcalinita: At pH above 7 (alkaline), keratin amino groups lose protons, generating a strong net negative electric charge. A phenomenon of electrostatic repulsion occurs between adjacent scales, causing:
en.sollevamento_cuticola: Cuticle Lifting: Scales open and the fiber swells (water absorption).
en.conseguenze_danno: Structural Degradation: Increased friction (frizz), high porosity, loss of mechanical strength, and greater susceptibility to breakage and scale removal during brushing.
en.desc_lista_ingredienti: Labeling must comply with Regulation (EC) No 1223/2009, using the International Nomenclature Cosmetic Ingredient (INCI) nomenclature. The order of declaration follows strict rules:
en.ordine_ingredienti: Descending Order of Weight: Ingredients are listed in order of weight at the time of addition to the formulation (from highest to lowest concentration).
en.ingredienti_meno_1: Below 1% Threshold: Ingredients with a concentration below 1% may be listed in arbitrary order after those above 1%.
en.posizione_coloranti: Colorants: Cosmetic colorants (excluding hair dyes) may be listed in arbitrary order at the end of the list, often identified by the Colour Index (CI) code.
en.desc_classi_ingredienti: Raw materials are classified based on their technological function in the formulation:
en.classe_emulsionanti: Emulsifiers: Stabilizers of biphasic systems (Oil-in-Water or Water-in-Oil).
en.classe_addensanti: Rheology Modifiers (Thickeners): Polymers and gums that modulate viscosity.
en.classe_umettanti: Humectants: Hygroscopic substances that bind water (e.g., Glycerin).
en.classe_lipidi: Lipids & Emollients: Vegetable oils, butters, synthetic esters.
en.classe_chelanti: Chelating Agents: Metal ion sequestrants (e.g., EDTA) to stabilize the formula.
en.classe_attivi: Active Ingredients: Functional ingredients with specific biological or cosmetic action.
en.subtitle_origine_materie_prime: Origin of Raw Materials and Formulation Philosophies
en.desc_origine_materie_prime: Cosmetic ingredients can be of mineral, animal, plant, or synthetic origin (laboratory-synthesized), which currently make up the majority of components to ensure stability and reproducibility.
en.desc_impostazione_usa: Historically favors stability, sensory pleasure, and cost-effectiveness, prioritizing high-performance synthetic or mineral ingredients.
en.desc_impostazione_europea: Tends towards an ecobio (ecological and organic) philosophy, with greater focus on sustainability, biodegradability, and toxicological safety (EU Reg. 1223/2009).
en.desc_ruolo_alcol: Denatured alcohol (ethanol with bittering agents such as denatonium) is a ubiquitous component with multiple technological functions:
en.alcol_solvente: Solvent: Dissolves resins, fats, and water-insoluble organic substances.
en.alcol_eccipiente: Excipient and Carrier: Facilitates the penetration of active ingredients into the shaft and provides a quick cooling sensation through evaporation.
en.alcol_antisettico: Antiseptic and Antifoaming Agent: Reduces microbial load and lowers viscosity in gel formulations.
en.desc_propellenti: Modern propellants used to atomize the product are light hydrocarbons (Butane, Isobutane, Propane), which replaced CFCs (chlorofluorocarbons) responsible for stratospheric ozone depletion. Although they do not damage the ozone layer, they are VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds).
en.desc_solventi_lacche: In hairsprays, alongside alcohols, volatile solvents (e.g., ethers or acetates) are used to facilitate rapid evaporation and film formation. Chlorinated solvents (e.g., dichloromethane) are now almost absent due to their high toxicity and regulatory restrictions.
en.additivo_vitamine: Vitamins: Niacinamide (Vitamin B₃) is a precursor of NAD/NADP coenzymes, essential for cellular energy metabolism and ceramide synthesis, strengthening the barrier and shine.
en.additivo_pantenolo: Panthenol: Provitamin B₅. It penetrates the shaft and enzymatically converts into pantothenic acid, a component of Coenzyme A. It improves hydration, elasticity, and mechanical resistance.
en.additivo_preservanti: Preservatives: Systems such as parabens (e.g., Butylparaben, limited to 0.14%) or Propylene Glycol (multifunctional glycol: humectant, solvent, and co-preservative).
en.desc_gel_lacche: Gels are water/alcohol-based fixatives with a high polymer content, while Mousses (foams) offer a lighter hold thanks to their aerated structure.
en.eccipienti_grassi: Fatty Excipients: Often plant derivatives (e.g., castor oil) to provide plasticity to the film and reduce the "crunchy" effect.
en.addensanti_gommina: Thickeners (Gums): Cellulose derivatives (Hydroxyethylcellulose, Hydroxypropylcellulose) increase the viscosity of the aqueous phase, stabilize the foam, and enhance the holding action.
en.caption_disposizione_silicone: Layered arrangement of PVP (inner) and polydimethylsiloxane (outer) on the hair.
en.subtitle_agenti_filmogeni: Film-Forming Agents and the Natural Effect
en.desc_filmogeni: Film-formers are polymers that form a continuous film on the shaft, providing body, texture, and smoothness. Technology has evolved from rigid polymers to hybrid systems.
en.desc_pvp: Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP): Historic hydrophilic polymer. It creates a rigid, crystalline film that is water-soluble. It is responsible for the classic wet look and the "crunchy" feeling under high humidity.
en.desc_sistemi_moderni: Modern Systems (PVP + Silicone): The addition of Polydimethylsiloxane (silicone) creates a hydrophobic outer layer that protects the PVP from atmospheric humidity. It maintains hold without the wet look, ensuring a natural appearance and brushability.
en.desc_copolimero_schiuma: In mousses, a silicone copolymer (e.g., Dimethicone Copolyol) is often used to stabilize the foam and aid distribution. The hydrophilic polyether component makes it soluble in water/alcohol, while the silicone part adds shine and reduces stickiness.
en.avvertenze_uso: Excessive use of gels or hairsprays, especially on fragile or chemically treated hair, can stiffen the shaft, making it susceptible to mechanical breakage. Aggressive removal (dry brushing) of polymer residues can cause Trichorrhexis Nodosa (shaft breakage with a "paintbrush" appearance). Fixatives should always be removed with a gentle wash in lukewarm water, never mechanically when dry.
en.desc_tinture: Coloration is based on the selective absorption of light by chromophore molecules. Dyes are classified based on their mechanism of action and persistence:
en.tintura_henne: Vegetable Dyes (e.g., Henna): Coloration by deposition on the cuticle (non-oxidative).
en.tintura_temporanea: Temporary Color: Large molecules that deposit only on the surface (removable with the first shampoo).
en.tintura_diretta: Direct Color (Semi-permanent): Small molecules that slightly penetrate the cortex without oxidation (lasts 6-8 washes).
en.tintura_demipermanente: Demi-permanent Color: Low-development oxidative system (low peroxide), penetrates the cortex without lightening natural hair (lasts 20-25 washes).
en.tintura_permanente: Permanent Color (Oxidative): Colorless precursors + oxidant (H₂O₂) + alkalizing agent. They form large, insoluble pigments inside the cortex. Stable color modification.
en.desc_henne: Unlike oxidative dyes, henna does not use aggressive alkalizing agents. Its active pigment, lawsone (2-hydroxy-1,4-naphthoquinone), diffuses through the cuticle and binds to keratin not just physically, but by forming a stable covalent bond via a Michael addition reaction with the hair's thiol and amine groups.
en.effetto_sostantivante_henne: Structural Effect: Creates a translucent protective layer that increases diameter, mechanical resistance, and shine, sealing the cuticle without damaging the internal protein structure.
en.benefici_henne: Beyond coloring, lawsone provides sebum-regulating, antifungal, and antibacterial properties, making it ideal for oily scalps or dandruff. Hair appears fuller and hydrated, with improved definition (especially in curls) and reduced sensitivity to external agents thanks to the barrier effect of the formed covalent bond.
en.desc_temporanea: Temporarily modifies the tone, providing only reflections. Does not cover gray hair.
en.meccanismo_temporanea: Mechanism: Pigments have a high molecular weight and deposit on the cuticle via physical absorption and electrostatic interactions without penetrating. They are removed in 1-2 washes.
en.composizione_temporanea: Composition: Absence of ammonia, alkaline bases, and oxidants. Uses direct dyes (azoic, anthraquinone) that adhere superficially to the fiber.
en.desc_semipermanente: Direct coloring that partially covers gray hair and lasts 4-6 weeks (approx. 6-12 washes). Has no lightening power.
en.penetrazione_semipermanente: Physical Penetration: Direct dyes (pre-formed molecules of medium size, e.g., azoic) penetrate the cuticle and superficial cortex by exploiting porosity or slight swelling. The pH is generally neutral to slightly acidic (5.5–7.0); no internal oxidation reaction occurs.
en.fissaggio_semipermanente: Fixation: Color is retained by Van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonds, and ionic interactions. The final rinse helps close the cuticle, physically trapping color molecules in the outer layers.
en.desc_demipermanente: Similar to semi-permanent but with deeper penetration into the cortex. Lasts up to 24-28 washes. Uses a mild alkaline agent (often ethanolamine) and a low-volume oxidant (1.9% - 4% H₂O₂). The pH (7.5–8.5) promotes controlled cuticle swelling for deeper fixation, sometimes with small amounts of resorcinol. Ammonia-free, it is ideal for toning, refreshing color, or correcting unwanted tones without significant lightening power.
en.desc_permanente: Durable technique that permanently modifies the hair's chromatic structure, allowing for lightening and complete coverage of gray hair.
en.fase_ossidazione: Alkalization/Oxidation Phase (pH ≈ 9–10): Ammonia (or MEA) swells the fiber, lifting the cuticles. Hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂, 3–9%) oxidizes and dissolves natural melanin (lightening) and activates precursors.
en.fase_cromogena: Chromogenesis Phase (Coupling): Small, colorless molecules (precursors, e.g., para-phenylenediamine) and couplers (e.g., resorcinol) penetrate the cortex. Oxidation transforms them into large pigment molecules (indosines) that remain stably trapped inside the fiber due to their size.
en.note_finale_permanente: A final acidifying treatment is crucial to close the cuticles and lock in the pigment. An incorrect process can leave the cuticle open, causing permanent porosity and structural damage.
en.title_ingredienti_critici: CRITICAL INGREDIENTS and Restricted Substances (EC Reg. 1223/2009) 🛑
en.desc_regolamento: EC Regulation No. 1223/2009 (and subsequent 2024-2025 updates) imposes severe restrictions or bans on substances that are potentially hazardous, carcinogenic, mutagenic, or endocrine disruptors (EDCs).
en.rischio_catrame_ppd: Risk: Coal tar is banned (Annex II). PPD (synthetic) is a potent allergen and skin sensitizer; while historically associated with systemic risks, it is permitted in hair dyes within strict limits.
en.normativa_catrame_ppd: Regulation: PPD is permitted only in hair dyes up to a maximum of 2% in the finished product. It is mandatory to include the warning: "Can cause an allergic reaction" and instructions for a sensitivity test. Use on eyelashes and eyebrows is prohibited.
en.rischio_amine_meateadea: Risk: Potentially toxic if contaminated. Secondary derivatives (like DEA) can react with nitrating agents to form nitrosamines (classified as possible IARC carcinogens).
en.normativa_amine_meateadea: Regulation: The EU bans the use of DEA and its derivatives in products containing nitrating agents. Nitrosamine concentration must be kept below 50 µg/kg, and the substance must be stored in nitrite-free containers.
en.rischio_sls_sles: Risk: Aggressive surfactants for the skin barrier. The main risk is not direct carcinogenicity, but possible contamination with 1,4-dioxane (byproduct of ethoxylation) and nitrosamine formation under specific conditions.
en.normativa_sls_sles: Regulation: The EU mandates strict purity controls to minimize the presence of 1,4-dioxane and other impurities in ethoxylated surfactants.
en.rischio_formaldeide: Risk: Highly irritating, sensitizing, and carcinogenic (Cat. 1B). Used as a preservative (releasers) or as an impurity.
en.normativa_formaldeide: Regulation: Free formaldehyde is banned above 0.05%. For formaldehyde releasers, the mandatory labeling threshold ("contains formaldehyde") has been drastically lowered to 0.001% (10 ppm) to protect sensitized consumers (EU Reg. 2022/2195).
en.rischio_parabeni: Risk: Preservatives suspected of weak estrogenic activity (endocrine disruptors). Long-chain parabens have higher bioaccumulation potential.
en.normativa_parabeni: Regulation: Five parabens (Isopropyl-, Isobutyl-, Phenyl-, Benzyl-, Pentylparaben) are BANNED. Those permitted (Methyl-, Ethyl-, Propyl-, Butylparaben) have a single limit of 0.4% and a total limit of 0.8%. For Propyl- and Butylparaben in leave-on products for the diaper area (children <3 years), use is prohibited.
en.rischio_petrolati: Risk: Petroleum byproducts. The theoretical risk is contamination with PAHs (Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons), known carcinogens, if not properly refined.
en.normativa_petrolati: Regulation: In the EU, they are permitted only if fully refined. The refining history must demonstrate the absence of carcinogenic properties and compliance with European Pharmacopoeia purity specifications (absence of PAHs).
en.rischio_toluene_triclosano_piombo: Risk: Toluene is toxic to reproduction. Triclosan is an endocrine disruptor and promotes antibiotic resistance. Lead is a banned contaminant (not an intentional ingredient).
en.normativa_toluene_triclosano_piombo: Regulation: Triclosan is subject to severe restrictions and, from October 31, 2025, will no longer be placed on the market in most cosmetics (EU Reg. 2024/996). 4-Methylbenzylidene Camphor (4-MBC) is banned from May 1, 2025.
en.rischio_peg: Risk: Used as emulsifiers and penetrants. The main risk is contamination from ethoxylation processes with 1,4-Dioxane (probable carcinogen) and Ethylene Oxide.
en.normativa_peg: Regulation: The EU requires PEGs and ethoxylated surfactants to undergo purification processes to reduce contaminants to the lowest technically feasible levels. The presence of 1,4-dioxane is strictly monitored.
en.desc_shampoo: Shampoo is a complex aqueous colloidal system. Its primary function is the mechanical removal of sebum and dirt through the synergistic action of surfactants, without altering the physiological barrier of the scalp.
en.desc_composizione_base: A standard formulation consists mainly of deionized water (70-80%) as a universal solvent and a surfactant system (10-15%) responsible for cleansing. Critical functional components are added for product stability and tolerability.
en.sistema_tensioattivo: Surfactant System: A balanced mixture of primary (cleansing) and secondary (mildness-boosting) surfactants. (See next table for chemical classification and charges).
en.agenti_chelanti: Chelating Agents (Sequestrants): Molecules like EDTA or citrates are essential to bind calcium and magnesium ions in hard water. Without them, surfactants would precipitate, reducing foam and leaving dull residues on hair.
en.regolatori_ph: Buffer System (pH): Additives like citric acid or lactic acid maintain the product at pH 4.5–5.5. This range is crucial to respect the scalp's acid mantle and keep hair cuticles closed and shiny.
en.sostanze_ispessenti: Rheology Modifiers: Salts (sodium chloride) or cellulosic polymers that increase solution viscosity, improving product handleability during application.
en.conservanti_shampoo: Preservative System: Necessary due to the high water content and presence of organic nutrients. Prevents microbial contamination (bacteria and mold) throughout the product's shelf life.
en.agenti_condizionanti: Conditioning Agents: Polymers or silicones that deposit a thin film on the fiber. (The mechanism of electrostatic deposition is covered in the next section).
en.additivi_sensoriali: Aesthetic Additives: Fragrances, dyes, and opacifying agents (e.g., glycol distearate) that provide the characteristic pearly appearance and scent, with no direct influence on cleansing.
en.a_uso: Primary cleansing and abundant foam. Can be aggressive if not balanced.
en.amf_uso: Foam boosters and mildness agents. At physiological pH (5.5), they reduce anionic irritation.
en.ni_uso: Ultra-mild cleansing, co-surfactants for sensitive skin. Excellent tolerability.
en.c_uso: Conditioners and antistatics. Non-cleansing: they bind to negatively charged keratin.
en.mic_p1: Surfactants are amphipathic molecules characterized by a dual chemical affinity:
en.mic_l1: Hydrocarbon Tail (Hydrophobic/Lipophilic): Affinity for fats; orients towards lipid dirt and sebum.
en.mic_l2: Polar Head (Hydrophilic): Affinity for water; ensures solubility of the complex in the aqueous phase.
en.mic_p2: Above a critical concentration threshold (CMC - Critical Micelle Concentration), molecules self-assemble into spherical aggregates called micelles. Hydrophobic tails protect themselves from water by orienting inward, while hydrophilic heads interact with the external solvent.
en.mic_p3: During washing, the lipophilic tail penetrates sebum, fragments it, and engulfs it in the micelle core (emulsification). The externally charged micelle is then rinsed away. At specific concentrations and in the presence of electrolytes, micelles can elongate into cylindrical structures ("worms"), increasing product viscosity.
en.sh_cur_p1: Special formulations containing specific active ingredients to normalize cell turnover or counteract microbial proliferation (Malassezia spp.).
en.sh_afor_d:
Use actives like Piroctone Olamine and Zinc Pyrithione (cosmetic) or Climbazole. In pharmaceutical contexts (prescription or OTC medicine), Ketoconazole or Selenium Disulfide are used.
Note: Coal tars are banned in the EU; vegetable oils (Cade, Birch) are used instead for keratolytic and anti-itch action.
en.sh_aseb_l1: Gentle Cleansers: Non-ionic or amphoteric surfactants (e.g., glucoside derivatives) that clean without excessively stripping the hydrolipidic film, avoiding reactive seborrhea.
en.sh_aseb_l2: Sebum-Regulating Actives: Ingredients like Zinc PCA or plant extracts (e.g., Serenoa Repens) that modulate 5-alpha-reductase activity, reducing sebum production.
en.sil_p1: Silicones (inorganic polymers based on silicon-oxygen, e.g., Dimethicone, Cyclomethicone) form a breathable occlusive film on the hair surface. This reduces friction (combing), adds shine, and protects from humidity. They are chemically inert, hypoallergenic, and non-irritating.
en.sil_p2:
The key distinction is between volatile (cyclic) and non-volatile (linear) silicones.
Regulation: The EU has restricted Cyclomethicone (D4, D5) and D6 in rinse-off products due to environmental persistence and bioaccumulation (PBT), not human toxicity.
Comedogenicity: Modern silicones (e.g., high molecular weight Dimethicone) are generally non-comedogenic as their molecules are too large to clog hair follicles.
en.ps_bal_p1: Impart elasticity, smoothness, shine, and manageability to hair, reducing static electricity. Ideal for long, dry, or damaged hair to prevent knotting.
en.ps_bal_l1: Composition: Self-emulsifying waxes (e.g., cetearyl alcohol), cationic surfactants (quaternary ammonium salts like Behentrimonium Chloride), silicones (dimethicone), hydrolyzed proteins, and film-forming polymers.
en.ps_bal_l2: Mechanism: Electrostatic bonding between the product's positive charges and the negative charges of damaged keratin.
en.ps_bal_l3: Persistence: Effect is reversible and gradually removed with subsequent washes.
en.ps_bal_l4: Contraindications: Not recommended for oily hair; may favor surface buildup (residue accumulation) and accelerate sebum migration.
en.ps_mas_p1: High-viscosity products applied post-shampoo and rinsed out (5-15 min). They provide intensive restructuring, nutrition, and hydration treatment.
en.ps_mas_s: Chemical Structure: Oil-in-Water (O/W) emulsions stabilized by non-ionic (polysorbates) or cationic (quaternary esters) emulsifiers.
en.ps_mas_w: Warnings: Excessive use can cause fiber weighing down, loss of volume, and a premature greasy sensation (greasy effect).
en.ps_fix_p1:
Leave-in products that act via film-forming synthetic polymers (PVP, Polyvinyl Acetate) to confer rigidity and hold to styling.
Note: Excessive application may cause flaking.
en.ps_tratt_p1: Rinse-free products applied to damp or dry hair to deliver specific active ingredients to the scalp or hair fiber.
en.ps_tratt_afor_d: Contain antimicrobial and keratolytic agents: Zinc Pyrithione, Octopirox (Piroctone Olamine), Selenium Sulfide, Coal Tar, or undecylenic derivatives.
en.ps_tratt_aseb_d: Aim to regulate sebum: Colloidal Sulfur, Coal Tar, plant extracts (Nettle, Sage, Aloe), or exfoliating acids (Salicylic Acid).
en.ps_tratt_acad_d: Formulations with DHT inhibitors (e.g., Serenoa Repens extract), follicle stimulants (Caffeine, Biotin, biomimetic peptides), and vasodilators.
en.pig_p1: Melanin, synthesized by melanocytes (dendritic cells of the basal layer), is the pigment responsible for skin phototype. Cutaneous dyschromias are qualitative or quantitative alterations of melanin, classified as hypochromias (defect) and hyperchromias (excess).
en.iper_p1: They can be generalized (e.g., Addison's disease, hemochromatosis, vitamin deficiencies) or localized.
en.ipe_ef_l1: Characteristics: Yellow-brown macules, flat, with blurred margins, typical of fair skin phototypes (I-II).
en.ipe_ef_l2: Etiology: Induced by UV rays, causing increased tyrosinase activity and melanosome size, without an increase in melanocyte number.
en.ipe_ef_l3: Course: Worsen in summer and regress in winter. They are not neoplastic lesions.
en.ipe_lent_l1: Characteristics: Flat macules (not raised), hyperpigmented, with sharp margins ('cookie-cutter'). Unlike ephelides, they persist year-round and are linked to photoaging.
en.ipe_lent_l2: Histopathology: Linear hyperplasia of melanocytes along the dermo-epidermal junction.
en.ipe_lent_l3: Treatment: Cryotherapy, Laser (Q-switched), or topical depigmenting agents.
en.ipe_nevi_l1: Characteristics: Benign proliferation of melanocytes ('nevus cells'). They can be junctional (flat), compound (slightly raised), or dermal (tuberous/papillomatous).
en.ipe_nevi_l2: Oncological Risk: Dysplastic nevi or atypical junctional nevi carry a higher risk of transformation into malignant melanoma.
en.ipe_nevi_w: Monitor: Asymmetry, irregular Borders, variable Color, Diameter >6mm, Evolution (rapid changes). Additional signs: bleeding, itching, erythematous halo (Meyerson's sign).
en.ipe_altre_l: Melasma (Chloasma gravidarum), Solar Lentigo, Post-inflammatory Hyperpigmentation, Tinea Versicolor (hyperchromic type).
en.ipo_p1: Conditions characterized by a reduction or absence of melanin.
en.vit_l1: Etiology: Chronic autoimmune pathology: selective destruction of melanocytes mediated by cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CD8+), with strong genetic predisposition.
en.vit_l2: Clinical Features: Acromic macules (milky white) with sharp margins, often symmetrical. Leukotrichia (white hair in the affected area) is frequent and indicates a reserved prognosis.
en.vit_l3: Management: Depigmented skin lacks natural UV protection (risk of sunburn and carcinomas). Rigorous photoprotection (SPF 50+) is required.
en.alb_l1: Etiology: Group of recessive genetic disorders characterized by absence or reduction of melanin synthesis. Melanocytes are present but functionally defective (enzymatic deficit, e.g., tyrosinase).
en.alb_l2: Clinical Features: White skin, white or straw-blonde hair, gray-blue or translucent irises (red reflex).
en.alb_l3: Ocular Complications: Foveal hypoplasia (underdevelopment of the fovea), nystagmus (involuntary eye movements), severe photophobia, strabismus, and low vision due to anomalous decussation of optic fibers.
en.ipo_altre_l: Pityriasis Alba (atopic dermatitis), Tinea Versicolor (hypochromic type), Nevus Anemicus, Idiopathic Guttate Hypomelanosis.
en.les_tit: PRIMARY AND SECONDARY SKIN LESIONS: Classification and Diagnosis 🔬
en.les_p1: Primary lesions constitute the fundamental morphological unit of the cutaneous response to endogenous or exogenous stimuli. Dermatological diagnosis relies on the accurate analysis of their morphology, topographic distribution, configuration, and arrangement.
en.les_prim_d: Represent the initial, unmodified manifestation of skin pathology. They appear on seemingly healthy skin (e.g., macule, papule, nodule, vesicle, bulla, pustule, wheal).
en.les_sec_d: Derive from the spontaneous evolution of a primary lesion or are induced by external factors (trauma, scratching, therapeutic treatments). Examples include: crust, scale, erosion, ulceration, fissure, scar, lichenification.
en.les_ps_d: Pathological conditions where primary lesions (active phase) and secondary lesions (evolutionary or regressive phase) coexist simultaneously. A classic example is eczema, where erythema and vesicles (primary) are associated with excoriations, erosions, and crusts (secondary).
en.eritema_d1: Vascular skin discoloration caused by vasodilation of the capillaries in the papillary dermis. Color ranges from bright red to purplish. Characterized by positive diascopy (blanches under vitropressure).
en.eritema_d2: Can be transient (physiological: heat, emotion) or persistent (pathological: rosacea, lupus). A hallmark sign of inflammation, infections, hypersensitivity reactions, or photodamage.
en.eritema_d3: Clinical presentations: Infectious exanthems, sunburn, rosacea, erythema multiforme. Morphologies: scarlatiniform, roseoliform, morbilliform.
en.mac_d1: Circumscribed change in skin color, flat surface (non-palpable). Characterized by negative diascopy (does not blanch under pressure), indicating a pigmentary or hemorrhagic alteration.
en.mac_l1: Hyperchromic: Increase in melanin (e.g., freckles, solar lentigo, melasma).
en.mac_l2: Hypochromic/Achromic: Reduction or absence of melanin (e.g., vitiligo, pityriasis versicolor).
